title: Quenching of Nonrelativistic p-Wave Spin Splitting by c-f Decoupling in CeNiAsO
authors: Xinnuo Zhang, Zhicheng Jiang, Shibo Shen, Jian Yuan, Junseo Yoo, Changyoung Kim, Mao Ye, Jishan Liu, Zhengtai Liu, Yanfeng Guo, Yilin Wang, Dawei Shen
abstract: The extending of spin-space group symmetries to coplanar antiferromagnets has predicted the emergence of odd-parity nonrelativistic spin splittings, making the identification of a practical $p$-wave magnet a central pursuit in spintronics. The layered heavy-fermion oxypnictide CeNiAsO has been widely regarded as the prototypical platform to verify this paradigm, as its commensurate coplanar magnetic configuration is theoretically expected to induce a robust $p$-wave band splitting. Here, we investigate the electronic structure of single-crystal CeNiAsO using ultra-low-temperature, high-resolution, and resonant angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES). Across the consecutive magnetic transitions into the ordered phases, our spectroscopic data reveal neither the expected band folding associated with a spin density wave nor any observable $p$-wave band splitting, demonstrating that the conduction bands retain full Kramers degeneracy. By tracking the temperature dependence of the Ce 4$f$ spectral weight via resonant ARPES, we find no evidence of coherent $c-f$ hybridization near the Fermi level within the magnetically ordered states, confirming that the Ce 4$f$ electrons operate in the localized limit. Our findings establish a clear many-body constraint on projecting real-space magnetic symmetries onto momentum-space electronic bands, demonstrating that geometric symmetry classifications constitute a necessary framework but are not a sufficient condition for nonrelativistic spin splittings in the presence of strong electronic correlations.
summary: This work experimentally investigates the predicted $p$-wave spin splitting in the correlated material CeNiAsO, which is theoretically expected based on magnetic symmetry. The authors report that ARPES measurements fail to detect this splitting, demonstrating that the strong electronic correlations force the Ce 4f electrons into a localized regime. This establishes a key many-body constraint: symmetry alone is insufficient to guarantee spin splitting if electron localization dominates the magnetic coupling.
main_problem: The paper investigates the theoretical prediction versus experimental observation of nonrelativistic p-wave spin splitting in the heavy-fermion oxypnictide CeNiAsO, challenging symmetry-based predictions for magnetism.
main_result: Experimental ARPES data show no evidence of the expected spin splitting or band folding across magnetic transitions, suggesting that the Ce 4f electrons remain localized and decouple from the conduction bands.
method: The study combines ultra-low-temperature, high-resolution ARPES measurements with theoretical calculations (DFT) comparing different treatments of the f-electrons.
model_or_system: The system is the layered heavy-fermion oxypnictide CeNiAsO, which is studied across magnetic transitions into SDW and coplanar antiferromagnetic states. The physics involves the interplay between itinerant conduction electrons and localized Ce 4f moments.
key_observables: ARPES band dispersions, Fermi surface mapping, observation of $p$-wave band splitting, and the spectral weight of the Ce 4f electrons.
important_parameters: Magnetic transition temperatures ($T_{N1} \approx 9 	ext{ K}$, $T_{N2} \approx 6 	ext{ K}$), the ratio of Kondo coherence temperature ($T^*$) to Néel temperature ($T_N$), and the localized vs. itinerant limit.
main_assumptions: The initial theoretical framework assumes that extending spin-space group symmetries predicts the splitting, but the experiment tests the validity of this assumption under strong correlations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate magnetic transitions via transport measurements, ARPES intensity maps showing the absence of predicted band folding or spin splitting, and theoretical comparisons between different electronic structure treatments.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the theoretical prediction based on symmetry, presents the experimental ARPES measurements across magnetic phases, analyzes the failure to observe the predicted splitting, and concludes by revising the theoretical understanding to incorporate the many-body constraint imposed by electron localization.
why_interesting: The core finding—that symmetry is necessary but not sufficient—provides a crucial lesson for modeling correlated electron systems, particularly those where local moments interact with itinerant carriers, which is relevant to understanding exotic quantum states in condensed matter.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic illustration of a universal one-dimensional model generating a p-wave magnet[25], where Jsd represents the local exchange coupling between the itinerant and localized spins on the same lattice site, t denotes the electron hopping matrix element between adjacent sites, and α indicates the propagation trajectory of the spiral configuration. (b) Schematic representation of the spin-polarized Fermi surfaces characteristic of a p-wave magnet, satisfying E(k, σ⊥) = E(−k, −σ⊥). (c) Crystal structure of tetragonal CeNiAsO highlighting the alternating stacking layered profiles. (d) Incommensurate spin- density-wave (SDW) state established below TN1, characterized by a collinear...

FIG. 2. (a) Schematic three-dimensional Brillouin zone of CeNiAsO and its projection onto the (001) surface Brillouin zone. (b) Schematic illustration of the cleaved CeNiAsO surface with possible terminations, together with spatially resolved As 3d core-level spectra measured on two representative surface domains. Gray curves denote the Lorentzian components used in the core-level fitting. (c) ARPES intensity plots and corresponding second-derivative images measured along the Γ-X direction on the two surface domains. The arrow marks an additional surface-related band observed on domain 1. (d) Photon-energy- dependent ARPES intensity map along the M-Γ-M direction, plotted as a function of...

FIG. 3. (a1)-(a3) Schematic Fermi surfaces expected for the paramagnetic phase, the spin-density-wave phase below TN1, and the proposed p-wave altermagnetic phase below TN2, respectively. Below TN1, the magnetic unit-cell doubling is expected to induce band folding along the Γ-X/Y directions. Below TN2, the proposed p-wave altermagnetic order would further lift the spin degeneracy, as illustrated by the red and blue contours. (b)-(d) ARPES Fermi-surface intensity maps measured at 13.5 K, 7 K, and 5.9 K, respectively. Red dots mark the Γ points, and red squares indicate the projected Brillouin zone. (e)-(g) Corresponding ARPES spectra measured along the Γ-X direction at the same temperatures...

FIG. 4. Resonant ARPES results and proposed mechanism for the quenching of nonrelativistic p-wave order. (a) ARPES intensity plot measured along the Γ–X direction at 6.5 K using a near-resonant photon energy of 118 eV. The dashed lines mark the boundary of the first Brillouin zone, and the solid line indicates the Fermi level. (b) ARPES intensity plot measured along the Γ–X direction at 6.5 K using a near-resonant photon energy of 122 eV. The vertical dashed lines mark the boundary of the first Brillouin zone, the horizontal dashed lines indicate the two resonant 4f levels, 4f 5/2 and 4f 7/2, and the solid line denotes the Fermi level.
